report:
FITNESS GOAL ANALYSIS REPORT
Goal Period: May 16 – June 11, 2026 (27 days)

────────────────────────────────────────

1. GOAL OUTCOME

No quantitative weight or body fat targets were set for this goal period. The plan defined input targets only — a 2,000 calorie daily ceiling and a 135g protein target (108g floor). Neither input target was consistently met, making this a period where the process itself fell short rather than a specific outcome.

Goal Met: 0 of 0 | Weight: gained 2.9 lbs (no target set) | Body Fat: lost 0.64% (no target set)

Note: Because no output targets were defined, this report focuses on input adherence, what the data reveals about body composition direction, and what drove each.

────────────────────────────────────────

2. THE NUMBERS

Start Weight:          123.2 lbs
End Weight:            126.1 lbs
Net Weight Change:     +2.9 lbs (gained)

Start BF%:             15.43%
End BF%:               14.79%
BF% Change:            -0.64% (directional improvement; see note below)

Start Lean Mass:       104.2 lbs
End Lean Mass:         107.5 lbs
Lean Mass Change:      +3.3 lbs

Avg Daily Calories:    1,548 (target: 2,000 — averaged 452 below)
Calorie Logging:       89% of days logged
Avg Daily Protein:     137.0g (target: 135g — met on average)
Protein at/above 135g: 58% of logged days
Protein at/above 108g: 92% of logged days

Total Workout Sessions: 14 across 27 days
Avg Sessions/Week:      3.6
Avg Strain Score:       19.23

BF% Note: BF% readings here are derived from a limited number of weekly datapoints and likely reflect a bioelectrical impedance or similar consumer-level method. Treat the -0.64% directional decline as encouraging but not precise. The lean mass gain of +3.3 lbs is notable and consistent with the BF% trend, but the combination of weight gain plus BF% drop could also reflect measurement variability. Do not over-index on the exact numbers.

────────────────────────────────────────

3. PROJECTION (IF GOALS MISSED)

No output targets were set, so there is no gap to close by a fixed deadline. However, the weight gain of 2.9 lbs across 27 days warrants context:

The rolling BF% data suggests this was largely lean mass accumulation rather than fat gain. If the goal going forward is fat loss, the current average calorie intake of 1,548 is well below the 2,000 ceiling, which sounds positive — but the extreme under-eating concentrated in week 2 (avg 932 cal/day) followed by periodic high-calorie spikes is a pattern that tends to complicate progress. If the user's actual intent was body recomposition, the trend is loosely favorable. If the intent was scale weight reduction, no current trajectory supports it. Fat loss is rarely linear, and the data here covers only 27 days with a significant gap in the middle — projections would not be reliable.

────────────────────────────────────────

4. WHAT DROVE RESULTS

The goal period broke into three distinct phases with meaningfully different input profiles, and the output data reflects each.

Phase 1 — May 16–22 (Week 1): Engaged baseline
Calories averaged 1,828/day. Protein averaged 146g/day. Five workout sessions logged, strain scores ranging 15–20. Weight moved from 123.2 to 124.6 lbs over the week — a modest uptick likely reflecting normal fluctuation and workout-driven water retention. BF% rolling average was 15.34%, declining slightly day-over-day from 15.57 to 15.17. This was the most consistent week of the period.

Phase 2 — May 23–27 (Week 2): Near-complete breakdown
May 23 logged only 260 calories and 39g protein — almost certainly an incomplete logging day or a day of near-zero intake. May 24–26 have no data at all (three consecutive unlogged days). May 27 shows a weight of 130.3 lbs — a 3.5 lb jump from May 22's 124.6 lbs with no intervening data. Zero workout sessions this week. Average calories for the days that were logged came to 932/day, and protein dropped to 113g — below the 135g target and barely above the 108g floor. This is the single most disruptive period of the goal. The 130.3 lb reading on May 27 is almost certainly inflated by travel, dietary changes (high sodium, different eating patterns), water retention, or a combination — not 3.5 lbs of tissue gained in 4–5 days.

Phase 3 — May 28–June 11 (Weeks 3–4): Recovery and recomposition
Weight declined from 130.3 back toward 125–126 lbs range over roughly 10 days as normal eating and training resumed. This trajectory — rapid weight drop following a high-weight spike — is consistent with shedding retained water and glycogen normalization, not actual fat loss during that window. By around June 6–8, something more interesting happened: BF% rolling average dropped from 15.7% (week 3) to 14.88% (week 4), while weight stabilized around 125–127 lbs. The 3–4 days prior (June 2–5) showed consistent moderate calories (1,454–1,813) and elevated protein (147–163g) alongside consistent workout sessions with strains of 19–24. This input cluster — moderate deficit, high protein, daily training — appears to coincide with the most meaningful BF% directional shift of the entire period, roughly 5–6 days later in the rolling average signal.

────────────────────────────────────────

5. WHAT HELD YOU BACK

The week of May 23–27 is the clearest breakdown point. Three consecutive days of missing data, zero workouts, and what appears to be a near-zero calorie day on May 23 followed by a 130.3 lb weigh-in on May 27 indicates a disruption — likely travel, illness, or an unplanned life event — that was not part of the plan. This single week erased the consistency built in week 1 and introduced a recovery tax (rehydration, glycogen refill) that consumed most of week 3.

Calorie floor compliance is also worth flagging. The average daily intake of 1,548 is 452 calories below the 2,000 target. While a calorie ceiling is a maximum, the pattern here is not controlled restraint — it's volatility. Days like May 21 (1,335 cal), June 1 (1,059 cal), and May 29 (1,120 cal) sit far below maintenance for someone training at this volume. Under-eating at this level while running high-strain workouts creates a recovery debt, which may explain why lean mass readings appear elevated (measurement noise from dehydration) and why weight fluctuations were so pronounced.

Protein adherence at the floor level (92%) was solid, but hitting the actual 135g target only 58% of days means there were meaningful protein gaps on nearly half of logged days.

────────────────────────────────────────

6. MEAL PATTERNS

Calorie volatility was the defining meal pattern issue. The range across logged days ran from 260 (May 23, almost certainly a logging failure or near-zero day) to 2,888 (May 30). Only a handful of days landed within a reasonable range of the 2,000 target — May 17 (1,879), May 20 (1,698), and May 31 (1,775) were the closest. Most days clustered either well below 1,700 or spiked above 2,200.

High-calorie days (above 2,000) occurred on: May 16 (2,576), May 22 (2,207), May 30 (2,888), June 6 (2,283). These are not tightly clustered but May 30 stands out — it was the day training resumed after the dropout week, and at 2,888 calories it was the highest single day of the period, coinciding with a strain of 29.27 (the highest of the period). That pairing is understandable but worth noting as a pattern: high-output days may be triggering overcorrection in eating.

The three-day logging gap (May 24–26) is the most significant data integrity issue. Whatever happened during those days drove the 130.3 lb weigh-in, and without data it cannot be analyzed. Eighty-nine percent logging compliance sounds reasonable, but those three consecutive unlogged days fell during the most consequential disruption of the goal.

────────────────────────────────────────

7. WORKOUT PATTERNS

Fourteen total sessions across 27 days at an average of 3.6 sessions per week is a reasonable frequency, but the distribution was uneven. Week 2 had zero sessions. Weeks 1 and 3 had five sessions each. Week 4 had four.

Sport mix was running-heavy: 14 running sessions out of the recorded breakdown, with 10 weightlifting sessions, 4 walks, 3 general activity, and 1 HIIT. This is a multi-modal week, though the sport counts appear to reflect the full period rather than unique days (sessions appear stacked on some days). Strain scores were mostly in the 17–21 range, with one notable outlier on May 30 at 29.27 — the first training day after the dropout week. Jumping to a peak strain day immediately after 7+ days off is a risk pattern for overreach and injury.

The final days of the goal (June 9–10) showed strain scores of 12.91 and 17.77 — lower than the preceding week — which may indicate appropriate taper, fatigue accumulation, or simply lighter activity choices. No sessions logged June 11 (final day), though calories were partially logged.

────────────────────────────────────────

8. LESSONS FOR NEXT GOAL

These are drawn directly from the patterns in this data:

One. Build in a contingency protocol for disruption weeks. The May 23–27 period destroyed weekly consistency and introduced recovery overhead that lasted into week 3. For the next goal, define in advance what minimum viable tracking looks like during travel or illness — even one weigh-in and one rough calorie estimate would have preserved continuity.

Two. The calorie target appears to be functioning as a ceiling you never approach, not a target you hit. Your average was 452 below the 2,000 cap. If 2,000 is genuinely the right number, you should be eating closer to it most days. If you naturally eat 1,500–1,700, consider whether the target should be reset to reflect actual behavior rather than an aspirational number you never reach.

Three. Protein floor compliance (92%) was the strongest input metric of the goal. Build on this — it was clearly the most sustainable target and likely contributed to the lean mass retention and BF% directional improvement. The 135g target was met only 58% of days; closing that gap is a more realistic lever than hitting a 2,000-calorie ceiling you consistently undershoot.

Four. Do not schedule a peak-strain workout as your first session back after an extended break. The May 30 session at strain 29.27 — after 7+ days off — is a setup for delayed-onset soreness, overreach, or injury. Next goal, plan the return-to-training day explicitly and keep it moderate.

Five. If the actual intent involves scale weight management, the current pattern of extreme undereating on some days offset by high-calorie spikes is working against you. The body composition signal (BF% down, lean mass up) is directionally positive, but it is happening alongside weight gain and significant measurement noise from hydration swings. Stabilizing daily intake closer to a consistent number would reduce this noise and make the signal more readable.
